coredata.dc:title: Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sung Jun
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
Moon, Byung Kee
coredata.dc:description: Our eyes are regularly exposed to ultraviolet (UV) and blue light emitting diode (LED) light-based devices. However, the blue light induces macular degeneration, optic nerve crush, eye strain, and increases reactive oxygen species, which negatively influence eye-related cells (photoreceptor and retinal pigmented epithelial cells). UV light is also harmful to humans. It induces photokeratitis, cataract, and ocular surface squamous neoplasia. Here, we present carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents, prepared by a simple method. The films exhibit strong UV and blue light absorption. The transmittance of carbon dot films is >70% in the visible region (>500 nm). The UV light blocking ratio of commercial blue blocking filter and carbon dot film using UV LED chips is 94.1% and 95.9% (40 wt%), respectively. The blue light blocking ratio of commercial blue blocking filter and carbon dot film using blue LED chips is 10.2% and 82.3% (40 wt%), respectively. These results indicate that the prepared carbon dot films have a UV blocking rate similar to that of commercial blue blocking filters and a much better blue blocking rate than commercial blue blocking filters. Therefore, they can be effectively used as UV and blue blocking films in various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
UV blocking film
Blue blocking film
White LED
Eye damage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of carbon dot and carbon dot film
Structural properties of the carbon dots
Optical properties of the carbon dots
Characterization of the carbon dot films
Potential applications: carbon dot films on UV, blue and white LEDs and blue LDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2017R1C1B1002434 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of problem of current WLEDs.
(a) TEM image, (b) FE-TEM image, (c) Particle size histogram, (d) XRD patterns, (e) Raman spectrum, (f) FT-IR spectrum, (g) XPS survey, (h) XPS c 1s, and (i) XPS O 1s spectra of carbon dots.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, (b) PL spectra of carbon dots upon excitation with different wavelengths. (c) Normalized PL spectrum, (d) Digital photographs of carbon dots under daylight, UV light and blue light.

(a) Photographs and SEM cross-section images of the PVA and carbon dot films prepared using different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%). (b) Absorption spectra, (c) Transmittance spectra of the PVA and carbon dot films prepared using different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%).

(a) Temperature-dependent PL spectra (λex = 460 nm) carbon dot film (40 wt%), (b) Activation energy for thermal quenching of the carbon dot film (40 wt%), (c) Corresponding temperature-dependent changes in the relative PL intensity, FWHM, and maximum λem. (d) PL intensity of carbon dot film as a function of heating-cooling cycles between 30 °C and 90 °C.

(a) Schematic of the experimental setup, (b) Blue InGaN chip spectra of glass, PVA film, commercial blue filter and carbon dot films prepared using different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%), (c) Photographs of InGaN LED, InGaN LED/glass, InGaN LED/filter, InGaN LED/PVA film, and InGaN LED/C-dot film (40 wt%), (d) Blue blocking ratio of glass, filter, PVA film, and carbon dot film (40 wt%).
(a) Scheme of the experimental setup, (b) Photographs of blue LD, blue LD/glass, blue LD/filter, blue LD/PVA film, and blue LD/C-dot films (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%).

Construction of LEDs (a), Electroluminescence spectra of InGaN LED/YAG film, InGaN LED/YAG film/filter, and InGaN LED/YAG film/PVA film (0 wt%) (b), InGaN LED/YAG film/C-dot film (10 wt%, c), InGaN LED/YAG film/C-dot film (20 wt%, d), InGaN LED/YAG film/C-dot film (30 wt%, e), InGaN LED/YAG film/C-dot film (40 wt%, f), and CCT (g), and CIE diagram showing the light coordinates of all fabricated LEDs (h).

head.ce:title: Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Our eyes are regularly exposed to ultraviolet (UV) and blue light emitting diode (LED) light-based devices. However, the blue light induces macular degeneration, optic nerve crush, eye strain, and increases reactive oxygen species, which negatively influence eye-related cells (photoreceptor and retinal pigmented epithelial cells). UV light is also harmful to humans. It induces photokeratitis, cataract, and ocular surface squamous neoplasia. Here, we present carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents, prepared by a simple method. The films exhibit strong UV and blue light absorption. The transmittance of carbon dot films is >70% in the visible region (>500 nm). The UV light blocking ratio of commercial blue blocking filter and carbon dot film using UV LED chips is 94.1% and 95.9% (40 wt%), respectively. The blue light blocking ratio of commercial blue blocking filter and carbon dot film using blue LED chips is 10.2% and 82.3% (40 wt%), respectively. These results indicate that the prepared carbon dot films have a UV blocking rate similar to that of commercial blue blocking filters and a much better blue blocking rate than commercial blue blocking filters. Therefore, they can be effectively used as UV and blue blocking films in various applications.

ce:section.ce:para: White-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) have recently gained much attention because of their compact volume, low heat generation, environmental friendliness, high efficiency, longer lifetime, and remarkable energy conservation [1–5]. WLEDs have been used as next-generation lighting technology for lighting systems and several applications such as computers and cellular phones [6–8]. Therefore, many nations have substituted general lighting technology with WLEDs. Generally, WLEDs are composed of a blue InGaN LED chip (∼460 nm); broad-spectrum yellow phosphor (YAG:Ce3+) or ultraviolet (UV) LED chip; and red, green, and blue phosphors. The intensity of the blue region is higher than that of the yellow region. However, there is a potential risk to human health. It is well known that blue light has a negative effect on the eyes including optic nerve crush, accumulation of eye strain, photoreceptor cell destruction, macular degeneration, and the decrease of retinal pigmented epithelium (RPE) melanin because blue light causes the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which induce oxidative stress, as shown in Fig. 1 [9–14]. To overcome this issue, many studies have been performed to cut the light in the UV and blue region.

Generally, absorptive or dichroic filters can be used to cut blue light. The light is blocked based on the absorption characteristics of the glass substrate used in the absorptive filter. In other words, it is not reflected by the blocked optical filter but is absorbed and stored in the filter. The advantage of this filter is that it is unaffected by the angles. The filter maintains its transmission and absorption characteristics, even if light enters at a wide angle. In contrast, a dichroic filter reflects unwanted wavelengths and transmits necessary portions of the spectrum. This effect is obtained by exploiting the interference characteristics of one or more additional light waves from the material layers with various refractive indices. In contrast to the absorptive filter, this method has the disadvantage of the angle being very sensitive, whereby it is impossible to block the desired wavelength, except for the designed angle. In consideration of these factors, we focused on the absorption of unwanted wavelengths (UV to blue region) and their conversion to long wavelengths to reduce the loss of light when blocking unwanted wavelengths. We also applied a method to maintain the blocking efficiency, regardless of the incident angle.

In recent years, carbon dots have attracted increasing attention as luminescent materials because of their unique properties such as tunable excitation, emission, high thermal stability, no optical blinking, and low toxicity compared with semiconductor quantum dots [15–18]. Traditional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., PbS and CdSe) contain heavy metals that are harmful to humans and environmentally hazardous [19,20]. Therefore, semiconductor quantum dots have been replaced by carbon dots. They have wide applications in many fields such as LEDs, bioimaging, catalysis, sensors, anti-counterfeiting, and solar cells [21–27].

In this paper, we report the fabrication of UV and blue-blocking and wavelength-convertible carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents (0, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) based on a simple solvothermal method. The carbon dot films measured the blue and UV blocking ratio through chip spectrum. The intensity of the UV and blue region gradually decreases and cool white-light emission changes to warm white light using films with different carbon dot contents (0, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%). To our knowledge, research on UV and blue-blocking and wavelength-convertible carbon dot films for the minimization of light loss, where the light is blocked by conversion from UV to blue to long wavelengths, has not been reported. The fabricated carbon dot films have a strong absorbance peak and strong blue excitation wavelength, which can diminish the intensity of harmful wavelengths reaching the eye. To verify the applicability of the carbon dot films, the blue region was confirmed to be reduced by depositing the films on UV LED, blue LED/LD, and white LED.

Acetone (20 mL) and deionized water (DI water, 20 mL) were filled in a beaker and stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 30 min. The obtained solution was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon liner (80 mL capacity and 50% filling), heated to 150 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min, and maintained for 10 h under magnetic stirring at 200 rpm. After the reaction, the autoclave was gradually cooled to 25 °C and the solution was filtered using a 0.2 μm membrane filter (CHM, Spain) to remove large aggregates. The product was purified using a 1000 Da dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da, Spectra/Por®6, nominal flat width 45 mm, diameter 29 mm, USA) for 24 h. The carbon dots were obtained through filtration using a 0.2 μm membrane filter.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, MW = 1500 g/mol) powders weighing 1.0 g were dissolved in 20 mL DI water under vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution was obtained, which was then cooled to RT. Appropriate amounts of PVA solution pour into five beakers. Subsequently, the different contents (0, 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) of prepared carbon dot solutions were added to the PVA solutions and the mixtures were stirred for 1 h to obtain homogeneous solutions, respectively. The solutions (PVA + carbon dot) were dropped into a petri dish with using a pipette and dried at 90 °C for 1 day. High-quality transparent and flexible films containing carbon dots with different carbon dot contents were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dot and carbon dot film
ce:section.ce:para: The size of carbon dots was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI LTD, H-7500) Lattice fringe images of the carbon dots were obtained via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). The structure of the carbon dots was analyzed with X-ray diffraction (XRD) using an X’PERT PRO X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (1.5406 Å) at a beam voltage of 40 kV and beam current of 30 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the carbon dots were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of carbon dots were conducted on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source. Raman spectra of the carbon dots were recorded with a micro-Raman spectroscope using a laser wavelength of 785 nm. The thickness of the carbon dot films was measured by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6700, JEOL). The UV–Vis spectra of the carbon dots and carbon dot films were measured with a V-670 spectrometer (JASCO). The luminescence properties of the carbon dots were measured at 30 °C using a luminescence spectrophotometer (Photon Technology International, PTI) and a 60 W Xe arc lamp. The thermal properties of the carbon dot film were investigated with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (DARSA PRO-5200) equipped with a 200 W Xe lamp as the excitation light source. Chip spectra of all fabricated LEDs were measured using an Ocean Optics detector (HR4000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the formation of carbon dots, FE-TEM images and the size distribution of the prepared carbon dots are displayed in Fig. 2 (a–c). The obtained particles have spherical shapes, as presented in Fig. 2(a). Fig. 2(b) and Figure S1 present the FE-TEM images, which demonstrate the high crystallinity of the carbon dots with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm and 0.41 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) peaks of graphitic carbon. The size of the obtained carbon dots is ∼5 nm, as displayed in Fig. 2(c).

The XRD patterns of the carbon dots show broad peaks at 2θ = 20.4° (d = 0.41 nm) and 2θ = 41° (d = 0.23 nm), which can be assigned to disordered carbon atoms with (002) and (100) planes, as shown in Fig. 2(d).

The Raman spectrum of the carbon dots shows two main peaks – the D band (sp3 defect) at 1374 cm−1 and the G band (sp2 carbon) at 1573 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2(e). The D band is attributed to vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice [28]. Generally, the presence of a defective carbon structure is low when the intensity of the G band is higher than that of the D-band. The IG/ID intensity ratio of the carbon dots is ∼1.1, indicating the high crystallinity of the carbon dots.

The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2(f) provides further structural insights and shows the surface functional groups of the carbon dots. It exhibits a broad and intense peak with maxima at 3421 cm−1, 2981 cm−1, 1741 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1478 cm−1, and 1045 cm−1, corresponding to the hydroxyl group (OH), CH, ester group (CO), CC, CO, and COC, respectively [29]. These results indicate that the carbon dots have various surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, ester, carbonyl, and acid groups.

The elements of the carbon dots were examined using the XPS spectrum. The carbon dots show two predominant peaks at 284.9 eV (C1s) and 532.6 eV (O1s), as depicted in Fig. 2(g). Their relative contents are 71.6% (C) and 28.4% (O) based on the calculations of the integral area. The C1s band in the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2(h)) was fitted using three carbon bonds: CC (sp2) at 284.7 eV, CO (sp2) at 286.5 eV, and OCO (sp2) at 288.2 eV [30]. The O 1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into two bands at 531.7 eV (CO) and 532.9 eV (CO), as shown in Fig. 2(i) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum of the carbon dots is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The absorption spectrum shows two intense peaks in the UV region centered at 242 nm, which are ascribed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, and 296 nm, which are ascribed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [32].

The PL spectra of the carbon dots display the excitation dependence of the emission and intensity, as shown in Fig. 3(b). When the carbon dots are excited at wavelengths between 380 and 520 nm in increments of 20 nm, the corresponding emission peaks are redshifted from 450 to 560 nm. The PL spectra of the carbon dots exhibit strong green fluorescence at 516 nm under 440 nm excitation. Fig. 3(c) depicts the normalized PL spectra of the carbon dots under 380–520 nm excitation. Photographs of the carbon dots taken under daylight, UV light, and blue light excitation are shown in Fig. 3(d). Light yellow, cyan, and green colors are visible, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the carbon dot films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) shows the photographs of the flexible PVA and carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) taken in the sunlight. The color of the carbon dot films gradually darkens with increasing carbon dot content. To confirm the thickness of the PVA and carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%), the cross-sectional FE-SEM images of the carbon dot films are analyzed (Fig. 4(a)). Although the content of the carbon dots increases, the thickness of the PVA and carbon dot films is similar (∼14–15 μm).

Fig. 4(b) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PVA and carbon dot films in the UV, visible, and IR range with increasing contents (10–40 wt%) of the carbon dots. In the case of the PVA film, only one absorption peak is observed. The PVA absorption peak exhibits a band centered at 260 nm [33]. When the film contains carbon dots, two absorption peaks appear. The first absorption peak of the carbon dot films is at ∼248 nm, which can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. The second peak of the n-π* transition (CO) appears at ∼313 nm. The absorption intensity of the carbon dot films increases with increasing carbon dot content. The transmittance of the PVA and carbon dot films at different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) is shown in Fig. 4(c). The transmittance of the PVA film is above 90% in the visible region. In the case of carbon dot films, the transmittance is above 70% (visible region), except for the blue region. The transmittance of the carbon dot films decreases with increasing carbon dot content. The inset in Fig. 4(c) represents the photographs of the PVA and carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents. The color of the PVA and carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) changes from white to dark brown. All fabricated samples are highly transparent.

To demonstrate the potential application and characterize the temperature-dependent PL properties of the carbon dot films, temperature-dependent PL spectra of the carbon dot film (40 wt%) were obtained at temperatures ranging from 30 °C to 90 °C (temperature interval: 15 °C) and 460 nm, as represented in Fig. 5 (a). Based on the temperature-dependent PL spectra, the intensity of the carbon dot films gradually decreases with the increasing temperature. There are many reasons for this such as non-radiative Auger scattering and the thermal activation of nonradiative trapping. In our experiment, the excitation density is fixed and very low; thus, Auger scattering is not the cause of the nonradiative relaxation. This indicates that the main factor for nonradiative relaxation is the thermal activation of nonradiative trapping. At low temperature, the nonradiative channel is not thermally activated. However, the non-radiative channels become thermally activated when the temperature increases [34,35]:
(1)
I=I
0 /[1 + Aexp(Ea/k
B
T)],
where I
0 is the initial intensity, I is the intensity at the given temperature T, Ea is the activation energy for thermal quenching, and k
B is the Boltzmann constant. Based on the Arrhenius equation, the activation energy Ea is obtained as 0.178 eV under 460 nm excitation, as shown in Fig. 5(b). This value is higher than those reported by Sadhanala (2018) and Kalychuk (2017) et al. [35,36], which indicates that the prepared carbon dot films have a good thermal stability and there are less surface/defect states compared with other carbon dots. Fig. 5(c) indicates the relative PL intensity, full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL spectra, and position of the maximum PL intensity of the carbon dot film (40 wt%) for each studied temperature. The relative PL intensity ratio of the carbon dot film (40 wt%) declines from 30 °C to 90 °C to approximately 54%. The FWHM of the PL spectra remains unchanged during temperature alteration. The result for the PL peak position of the carbon dot film (40 wt%) shows that the temperature does not affect the position of the maximum PL intensity. This means that the temperature does not alter the band gap of the carbon dots. To test the thermal stability of the carbon dot films, the PL intensity was measured for five continuous cycles of heating–cooling in the temperature range from 30 °C to 90 °C, as shown in Fig. 5(d). No thermal hysteresis was observed during several heating–cooling cycles, indicating that the PL intensity of the carbon dot film (40 wt%) has excellent thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications: carbon dot films on UV, blue and white LEDs and blue LDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability of the carbon dot films as blue light blocking films, the spectra of a blue InGaN LED chip (9 W, 460 nm), InGaN LED/glass, InGaN LED/filter (commercial blue blocking filter, HD.CRYSTAL), InGaN LED/PVA film, and InGaN LED/C-dot films (10–40 wt%)) were measured, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The chip spectra show that the glass, filter, and PVA film are hardly blocked in the 460 nm region, as depicted in Fig. 6(b). The higher the content of the carbon dots in the film, the higher is the blocking ratio in the 460 nm region. The blocking ratio of the glass, filter, and carbon dot films is 7.4% (glass), 10.2% (commercial blue blocking filter), 10.8% (PVA film), 25.2% (C-1 film), 46.5% (C-2 film), 53.8% (C-3 film), and 82.3% (C-4 film), as shown in Fig. 6(c). This indicates that the blocking ratio of the carbon dot films is higher than that of the commercial blue blocking filter, specifically, 2.47 (C-1 film), 4.56 (C-2 film), 5.27 (C-3 film), and 8.07 (C-4 film) times higher. The intensity of the chip spectra in the green region gradually increases when the content of the carbon dot films increases. Fig. 6(d) shows photographs of the glass, filter, PVA film and carbon dot film (40 wt%) on InGaN LED. The photographs of the glass, filter, and PVA film on the InGaN LED show that the visible intensity of the blue light is almost unchanged. In the case of the carbon dot film (40 wt%), the blue light intensity of the InGaN LED is weaker than that of the glass, filter, and PVA film. In addition, the blocking ratio of the glass, PVA film, and carbon dot films was further investigated under UV light. Generally, it is well known that UV light causes potentially lethal damage to the human eye such as photokeratitis, pterygium, cataract, climatic droplet keratopathy (CDK), and ocular surface squamous neoplasia (OSSN) [37–41]. Therefore, UV light is also harmful to people, similar to blue light, and should be blocked. The spectra of a UV LED chip (3 W, 370 nm), UV LED/glass, UV LED/PVA film, UV LED/C-dot films (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%), and UV LED/filter (commercial blue blocking filter) were measured using the same method as mentioned above (Figure S3(a)). The chip spectra show that the glass and PVA film are hardly blocked in the 370 nm region, as depicted in Figure S3(b). The UV blocking ratio increases with increasing carbon dot content in the film. The blocking ratio of the glass, PVA film, carbon dot films, and filter is 3.6% (glass), 31.7% (PVA film), 51.4% (C-1 film), 85.8% (C-2 film), 93.8% (C-3 film), 95.9% (C-4 film), and 94.1% (commercial blue blocking filter), as shown in Figure S3(c). Under UV light, the blocking ratio of the filter is almost 100%. The blocking ratio of the carbon dot films (30, and 40 wt%) is similar to that of the commercial blue blocking filter.

Photographs of the blue laser diode (LD < 200 mW, 405 nm), blue LD/glass, blue LD/filter (commercial blue blocking filter), blue LD/PVA film, and blue LD/C-dot films (10, 20, 30, and 40 wt%) were obtained using the method described in Fig. 7 (a). Fig. 7(b) shows the photographs of the glass, filter, PVA film, and carbon dot films excited with a blue LD. In the case of the glass and PVA film, the intensity of the transmitted blue light is similar to that of the blue LD. However, the intensity of the transmitted blue light using a filter is very weak because most of the blue light is blocked. In the case of the carbon dot films, the intensity of the transmitted blue light gradually diminishes and is converted to long wavelengths with increasing carbon dot content, which can be seen with the naked eye. The amount of blue light transmitted through the carbon dot film with a contents of 30 wt% or more is remarkably reduced. In particular, blue light can be completely blocked by using the carbon dot film (40 wt%).

To confirm the applicability of carbon dot films for general lighting, we fabricated white LEDs including a blue InGaN LED chip, YAG:Ce3+ film, and blue blocking filters (commercial filter, PVA film, and carbon dot films) and measured their chip spectra, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Fig. 8 presents the chip spectra of the (b) InGaN LED chip/YAG film, InGaN LED chip/YAG film/filter, InGaN LED chip/YAG film/PVA film (0 wt%), (c) InGaN LED chip/YAG film/C-dot film (10 wt%), (d) InGaN LED chip/YAG film/C-dot film (20 wt%), (e) InGaN LED chip/YAG film/C-dot film (30 wt%), and (f) InGaN LED chip/YAG film/C-dot film (40 wt%). The photographs showing the light emission of the fabricated LEDs are shown in the insets of Fig. 8(b–f). There is almost no difference in the blue light intensity of LEDs using commercial filter or PVA film compared to that without the film, as depicted in Fig. 8(b). For the carbon dot films, the blue region decreased compared with the yellow region with increasing content of the carbon dots, as depicted in Fig. 8(c–f). This leads to the decrease of the correlated color temperature (CCT) value due to the decrease of the blue area, as shown in Fig. 8(g). Therefore, the LED light color gradually changes from cool to warm white. However, the CCT value of the fabricated LEDs using a filter and PVA film almost does not change because of the low blue blocking ratio. The CCT value drastically changes as carbon dots are added. Fig. 8(h) shows the CIE coordinates of all fabricated LEDs. The chromaticity coordinates (x, y) of seven different films are: point 1: (0.284, 0.328), point 2: (0.290, 0.330), point 3: (0.291, 0.341), point 4: (0.326, 0.350), point 5: (0.350, 0.419), point 6: (0.369, 0.465), and point 7: (0.412, 0.492). Based on these results, we confirm that carbon dot films can be applied to UV or blue-based general lighting to block the UV to blue region, which is harmful to the human eye.

In summary, we synthesized carbon dots by using DI water and acetone, and carbon dot films with different carbon dot contents, via a simple method. The fabricated carbon dot films show absorption in the UV and blue region due to π-π* and n-π* and main excitation in the blue region. With increasing carbon dot content, the film absorption intensity in the UV and blue region gradually improves. Based on the above-mentioned characteristics, some of the blue light is blocked through absorption and some blue light is converted to green light, which increases the blocking rate. All carbon dot films maintain a light transparency of more than 70% in the visible region (>500 nm). The UV blocking ratio of the carbon dot films is higher than that of the commercial blue filter (C-4-film
≈
commercial blue filter > C-3 film > C-2 film > C-1 film) under 370 nm excitation. The blue blocking ratio of the carbon dot films is higher than that of the commercial blue filter (C-4 film > C-3 film > C-2 film > C-1 film > commercial blue filter) under 460 nm excitation. Illuminating the fabricated LEDs leads to different blue region intensities, CCT, and color depending on the film types and contents of the carbon dot film. The optical properties in terms of the color chromaticity coordinates (x, y) and CCT of all fabricated LEDs can be tuned from cool white [(0.284, 0.328), 8240 K, no film] to warm white [(0.412, 0.492), 4343 K, C-4 film]. Therefore, we conclude that carbon dot films are useful for various applications that require the cutoff of UV or blue light to prevent eye damage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (2017R1C1B1002434).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nanoen.2019.03.043.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sung Jun Park received his M.S. in 2017 from Pukyong National University. He is currently pursuing for his Ph.D. under supervision of Profs. Hyun Kyoung Yang in School of LED convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University. His current research interests are in the area of functional inorganic and organic materials (e.g., phosphors, biomaterials, photocatalyst materials, and carbon dots).

Hyun Kyoung Yang is a professor at LED convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Physics at Pukyong National University. In 2012, he joined the Korea Institute of Energy Research (KIER). In 2014, he now is guiding research group of around 6 people at Pukyong University aiming at developing functional materials and ceramics (e.g., composite materials, luminescent materials, phosphor in glass, and heat sink).

Byung Kee Moon is a professor in department of Physics at Pukyong National University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Physics at Pusan National University. His research interests are in the area of nanostructured inorganic and organic materials and their optical properties including their synthesis, structural and microscopic analysis.
